Long ago, before the stars were pinned to the velvet sky, the Moon was a lonely silver coin at the bottom of a dark ocean. The Sun, a boastful king of fire, ruled the heavens alone, burning so brightly that the world below was scorched and barren. He refused to share the sky, fearing his brilliance would be diminished.

One day, a small, courageous bird named Kestrel dove into the ocean's depths. Kestrel sought water to cool her burning nest but found the glowing coin instead. Entranced by its gentle, cool light, she grasped the Moon in her talons and flew upward. The weight was immense, dragging her feathers down, but she persisted, climbing higher until she breached the atmosphere.

The Sun, furious at the intrusion, hurled lances of heat at her. Kestrel dodged and weaved, her feathers singed black, but she did not drop her prize. With a final, desperate thrust, she pinned the Moon against the dark fabric of the night, opposite the Sun’s throne.

Exhausted, Kestrel fell back to earth, but the Moon remained. It offered a cool respite from the Sun’s tyranny, allowing life to grow in the quiet dark. To this day, the Kestrel hovers in the air, watching the sky, proud of the balance she brought to the world.

In the time before time, when the sky was a heavy blanket of grey slate resting upon the mountains, the world was silent. There was no wind to rustle the leaves, and no birds to sing the dawn. The great spirit Orok lived in the deepest cave of the highest peak. Orok was lonely, for his voice echoed endlessly against the stone, unanswered.

One day, Orok wept a single tear of pure silver. It rolled from his cheek, down the mountain path, and shattered against a jagged rock. From the shards of this tear, the First Voices were born—tiny, shimmering spirits of sound. One shard became the Whisper of the Grass, another the Roar of the Ocean, and the brightest shard became the Laugh of the Child.

Delighted, Orok breathed upon them, pushing them across the stagnant earth. His breath became the Wind, carrying the Voices to every corner of the land. The sky, startled by the sudden cacophony of life, lifted higher and higher to listen, turning blue with wonder. To this day, when the wind howls through the canyons, it is merely Orok, still breathing life into the world, listening joyfully to the song he created from sorrow.
